title: Building a Clean, Secure, and Scalable Go Web API: A Layered Architecture Guide
subtitle: In the previous post, a Swagger file was created to provide clearer documentation. At this stage, the focus is on designing a product…
excerpt: In the previous post, a Swagger file was created to provide clearer documentation. At this stage, the focus is on designing a product…

content.html: In the previous post, a Swagger file was created to provide clearer documentation. At this stage, the focus is on designing a product structure to outline the project. The main objective is to implement CRUD functionality in the admin panel and define the required Golang structures.

In this article, we’ll walk through a real-world example of how to structure a Go web application using layered architecture, and solve a common but critical problem: showing different data to different users — like Admins vs. Clients — without compromising security or maintainability.

content.alt: Building a Clean, Secure, and Scalable Go Web API: A Layered Architecture Guide

content.html: Imagine you’re building an e-commerce backend with two types of users:

items: Admins: Need full access to product details (price, cost, inventory, creation date).
Clients: Should only see public info (name, ID, selling price).

content.html: How do you:

items: Structure your code cleanly?
Prevent sensitive data from leaking to clients?
Avoid code duplication and tight coupling?

content.html: Let’s explore the journey from a basic handler-based app to a clean, layered, and secure Go API.
A clean Go project should separate concerns into distinct layers:

content.code: internal/├── model/       → Data structures (e.g., Product)├── repository/  → Data access (DB, in-memory store)├── service/     → Business logic├── controller/  → HTTP handlers└── dto/         → Data Transfer Objects (for API responses)

content.html: This follows the Hexagonal or Clean Architecture principles, where:

items: Lower layers don’t depend on higher ones.
The domain model (Product) is the single source of truth.

content.text: ❌ Common Mistake: Defining Product in Multiple Layers

content.html: A tempting but dangerous shortcut is to define Product separately in service and repository:

content.code: // ❌ In service/product.gotype Product struct {    ID    string    Name  string    Price float64}// ❌ In repository/product.gotype Product struct {    ID    string    Name  string    Price float64}

content.html: Even if they look identical, Go treats them as completely different types.

items: You can’t pass repository.Product to service.Product directly.
Manual mapping is required → boilerplate code.
If you add a field (e.g., Category), you must update both structs.
Risk of inconsistency and bugs.
Tight coupling between layers.

content.html: This violates the DRY principle and makes your app fragile.
Define Product once, in internal/model/product.go:

content.code: package modeltype Product struct {    ID        string    `json:"id"`    Name      string    `json:"name"`    Price     float64   `json:"price"`    CostPrice float64   `json:"-"`  // Internal field    Inventory int       `json:"-"`  // Hidden by default    CreatedAt time.Time `json:"created_at"`}

content.html: Now, all layers are imported and used model.Product:

✅ No duplication
✅ No mapping
✅ One change updates all layers

content.text: 🔐 Step 2: Control What Data Is Exposed

content.html: Now comes the real challenge: admins see everything, but clients see only public fields.
You might be tempted to use JSON tags like json:"-" or conditionally omit fields — but that’s error-prone and hard to test.

content.text: ✅ Best Practice: Use DTOs (Data Transfer Objects)

content.html: Create response-specific structs in internal/dto/product_dto.go:

content.code: package dtotype ClientProductResponse struct {    ID    string  `json:"id"`    Name  string  `json:"name"`    Price float64 `json:"price"`}type AdminProductResponse struct {    ID        string    `json:"id"`    Name      string    `json:"name"`    Price     float64   `json:"price"`    CostPrice float64   `json:"cost_price"`    Inventory int       `json:"inventory"`    CreatedAt time.Time `json:"created_at"`}

content.html: And add helper functions to convert:

content.code: func ToClientProduct(p model.Product) ClientProductResponse { ... }func ToAdminProduct(p model.Product) AdminProductResponse { ... }

content.html: This ensures:

items: The same domain logic is used everywhere.
The output is controlled per audience.
No risk of accidentally exposing CostPrice to clients.

content.text: 🔄 Layered Flow: From DB to HTTP

content.html: Here’s how data flows through the system:

content.code: Database/In-Memory       ↓repository → Returns model.Product       ↓service    → Applies business rules       ↓controller → Converts to dto.AdminProductResponse or dto.ClientProductResponse       ↓HTTP Response (JSON)

content.html: Each layer has a clear responsibility:

items: Repository: Load/save data.
Service: Validate, compute, orchestrate.
Controller: Handle HTTP, choose DTO, send JSON.

content.html: Go’s simplicity can tempt us to skip structure — but as your app grows, good architecture saves time, reduces bugs, and improves security.

items: Define domain models once — in internal/model.
Never duplicate structs across layers.
Use DTOs to control API output for different audiences.
Keep the service layer clean — it should use model.Product, not define it.
Let controllers handle presentation, not business logic.

content.html: Instead of separating by role, use DTOs and controllers to handle role-based views — but keep the core in the domain.
Example: product The module supports both roles

content.code: internal/product/├── model/│   └── product.go              → Full domain model├── dto/│   ├── client_product.go       → Public fields│   └── admin_product.go        → All fields├── controller/│   ├── client_controller.go    → Returns ClientProduct│   └── admin_controller.go     → Returns AdminProduct└── service/    └── product_service.go      → Business logic (used by both)

content.html:
✅ Same service
✅ Same model
✅ Two DTOs + Two controllers
But one source of truth.

go-simple/
├── main.go
├── internal/
│ └── health/
│ └── controller/
│ └── health_controller.go

content.alt: Building a Clean, Secure, and Scalable Go Web API: A Layered Architecture Guide
